step_by_step_reasoning: The architecture is split into ingestion, pre-processing, and post-processing phases. First, 100k IoT sensors (UserConsumerIOT) send water usage and alarm data through API Gateway. A Transformer Lambda standardizes the multi-protocol payload, splitting it into SQS queues for Flow and Alarm data. A Preprocessor Lambda queries ElastiCache Redis to determine which metrics need processing, filtering out redundant records. The remaining 40% of data is sent to a Post-Processor EC2 instance, which saves time-series events into DynamoDB and aggregated daily stats into Aurora. Finally, end-user mobile devices consume this data via APIs.

graph.name: Vigod Aqua Water Management Platform
graph.notes: An IoT utility monitoring architecture that scales to handle 27 million daily events while reducing costs by 25% through SQS buffering, smart caching, and serverless ingestion.

nodes.name: Sensors
nodes.notes: 100k field water meters transmitting utility data.

nodes.name: API Gateway
nodes.notes: Entry point for incoming utility data stream.

nodes.name: Transformer
nodes.notes: Standardizes raw data from multiple generations of sensors.

nodes.name: Preprocessor
nodes.notes: Processes queued records and decides which data warrants persistence.

nodes.name: Elastic Cache
nodes.notes: Redis cache used by the preprocessor to filter redundant data.

nodes.name: Post Processor
nodes.notes: Processes the filtered 40% of data stream.

nodes.name: DynamoDB
nodes.notes: Stores event data such as tank deliveries and pump events.

nodes.name: Mobile Client
nodes.notes: End users accessing visualization reports and portals.

edges.notes: Sensors transmit usage and alarm metrics.
Pushes normalized flow data to queue.
Pushes normalized alarm data to queue.
Consults Redis cache to filter redundant data points.
Passes filtered 40% of events for final processing.
Feeds mobile apps with event logs.
Feeds dashboards with daily usage aggregates.
